U.S. stock index futures rallied Wednesday night, led by technology and semiconductor shares after Micron and Qualcomm issued strong AI-driven outlooks. Nasdaq 100 futures jumped 1.9%, while S&P 500 and Dow futures gained 0.5% and 0.1%, respectively. 
Micron surged more than 16% in after-hours trading after posting strong quarterly results and guidance, citing robust AI and data center demand. Qualcomm jumped 13.3% after projecting $15 billion in data center revenue by 2029, reinforcing optimism around AI-related growth. 
The upbeat forecasts sparked broad gains across the chip sector, with Seagate, Western Digital and SanDisk rising over 8%, while Arm, Intel, AMD, Broadcom and Nvidia also advanced. The rebound helped ease concerns that AI-driven valuations in the semiconductor industry had become overstretched following a sharp selloff earlier in the week.

$MU feels like one of those names where the cycle is doing most of the heavy lifting. Memory strength has been real, but I get the idea of not going all-in and spreading exposure across the space. $HON adds a different angle with aerospace optionality, while $STX feels more like a dividend/cash-flow play into the same memory theme. At this point it’s less about one winner and more about balancing how the cycle pays out across different setups.

When $MU kicked off the memory wave in mid/late 2025 you could’ve missed it and still made money by looking at the rest of the sector.

AMD acquires MEXT to advance memory optimization 
 
"Modern data center infrastructure is evolving rapidly, and customers are increasingly facing a common challenge: access to memory. As AI models, data analytics, virtualization and high-performance computing workloads grow in size and complexity, memory has become a critical constraint across cloud and enterprise environments. For customers, addressing these bottlenecks is essential to improving performance per dollar, increasing efficiency and accelerating deployments at scale. AMD is addressing this challenge by acquiring MEXT, a pioneer in AI-driven memory optimization technology.  
 
MEXT has developed innovative AI-powered predictive memory technology designed to make flash behave more like DRAM, helping expand usable memory capacity while maintaining performance and efficiency. This approach has the potential to reduce infrastructure costs, improve resource utilization, and help customers more effectively scale general-purpose and AI workloads... Demand for memory is growing across every category of enterprise compute. By combining the AMD leadership in high-performance computing and data center platforms with MEXT's memory optimization technology, we are taking another step to help customers deploy workloads more efficiently, cost-effectively and at greater scale," AMD announced.

AI is fueling massive demand for memory, benefiting Seagate, Western Digital, and Micron Technology, dubbed the “Suddenly Sexy 3.” In 2025, these companies delivered extraordinary stock returns—Seagate +219%, Western Digital +282%, and Micron +239%—as AI workloads drove surging demand for storage and high-performance memory. 
Seagate and Western Digital focus on nonvolatile long-term storage (HDDs and SSDs), while Micron produces DRAM and high-bandwidth memory (HBM) for AI applications. AI-driven data growth—from cloud computing to autonomous vehicles—is causing a boom in both capacity and pricing, with Micron projecting HBM market growth from $35B to $100B by 2028. Analysts expect tight supplies and strong margins to continue in 2026, keeping these stocks in focus despite historically cyclical swings in memory demand.

AI-driven data growth has transformed Seagate, Western Digital, and Micron from deep-value laggards into the top-performing stocks of 2025, far outpacing the Magnificent Seven. Demand for AI workloads is pushing unprecedented needs for both high-capacity storage and advanced memory, driving record profits and margins. 
Seagate and Western Digital benefit from surging demand for large-capacity hard disk drives used by hyperscale data centers, while Micron is capitalizing on explosive growth in high-bandwidth memory (HBM), a critical component for AI systems. Micron expects the HBM market to grow roughly 40% annually through 2028, potentially reaching $100 billion. 
Unlike past memory booms that ended in oversupply and price crashes, today’s industry is highly consolidated. A small number of players dominate hard drives and DRAM, and management teams have signaled discipline in capacity expansion, focusing instead on efficiency and density gains.

$STX.X 👑 Core v30 is Bullish for Stacks (STX) 🚀
The Bitcoin Core v30 release removes the 80-byte default limit on OP_RETURN. This is a major win for $STX for three core reasons:
 * Direct De-Risking: Stacks' Proof-of-Transfer (PoX) consensus uses OP_RETURN to write block hashes to Bitcoin. This change standardizes and future-proofs that core security mechanism.
 * Validation: Core v30 affirms Bitcoin's role as a neutral, reliable Data Availability layer, confirming the multi-layer strategy Stacks pioneered.
 * Efficiency: It favors OP_RETURN because its outputs are prunable, the most efficient way for Stacks to securely anchor to the main chain.
TL;DR: The v30 upgrade makes Stacks' essential connection to Bitcoin robust and officially supported—a powerful endorsement for $STX.

$STX  margin for HAMR is lower due to high cost. Heat is an issue and durability is suspect for HAMR

$STX What are the trade-offs / what limits remain

All new technologies have trade-offs. For HAMR, some of the challenges or current imperfect points are:

Power / heat / complexity: Having a laser and heating cycles adds complexity. There could be more challenges in manufacturing, thermal management, and long-term reliability of the media/head due to repeated heating. Seagate claims to have done a lot of testing, but real-world, long-term usage will still reveal issues. 

Random write performance sometimes drops: In comparing older (non-HAMR) high capacity drives with the new HAMR ones, there’s evidence the random write IOPS or latency is slightly worse (or at least not significantly better). The overhead for write calibration etc. is more with HAMR. 

Cost: New processes, new materials (for the head, media, laser integration) mean higher R&D and initially higher costs (both unit cost and yield). Over time this tends to improve. Seagate is pushing to make HAMR mainstream in high-capacity nearline / data centre drives, where cost per TB is a big driver. 

Scaled maturity: While prototypes, lab tests, and early units are promising, scaling to very high capacities (40-50-100 TB) with good cost, reliability, and performance is still in progress. Seagate has roadmaps.

Computer hardware and storage stocks rallied Thursday, led by SanDisk, which jumped 13% on expectations of gains from new export regulations. Western Digital rose 5%, while Seagate Technology, Micron, and Lam Research each advanced about 3%. 
Analysts highlighted that restrictions on memory chip production in China could disrupt supply and lift flash memory prices. Lynx Equity’s KC Rajkumar called SanDisk and Micron the “clear winners,” noting SanDisk’s focused flash-storage business model gives it a particular edge. Lam Research was also seen as well-positioned to benefit. 
The export rules are expected to constrain output at Samsung and SK Hynix’s China-based NAND plants, tightening supply and creating favorable pricing conditions for competitors with operations outside China.
